Description: Install Prisma CLI and client, then run prisma init to scaffold the project with a schema file, .env for DATABASE_URL, and generated client output. Always add prisma generate to your postinstall script.
Do: Run npx prisma init and configure the datasource provider and output directory in schema.prisma
Dont: Skip prisma init and manually create schema files or forget to set up the .env file

Guideline: Singleton PrismaClient pattern for connection reuse
Description: PrismaClient manages a connection pool internally. Creating multiple instances exhausts database connections. Use a singleton pattern that survives hot module reloading in development.
Do: Create a single PrismaClient instance stored on globalThis and reuse it across the entire application
Dont: Instantiate a new PrismaClient in every file or function or API route handler

Guideline: Model definition with proper field types
Description: Define models with explicit field types, attributes (@id, @unique, @default, @updatedAt), and database indexes. Use cuid() or uuid() for IDs, DateTime for timestamps, and add @@index for frequently queried fields.
Do: Use explicit scalar types with @id @unique @default attributes and add @@index on foreign keys and frequently filtered columns
Dont: Use ambiguous field names or omit indexes on foreign key columns and frequently queried fields
Code Good:
// schema.prisma
model User {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  email     String   @unique
  name      String
  bio       String?
  age       Int?
  isActive  Boolean  @default(true)
  credits   Decimal  @default(0)
  posts     Post[]
  profile   Profile?
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt

  @@index([email])
  @@index([isActive, createdAt])
}

Description: Define 1:1, 1:N, and N:M relations using @relation with fields and references. Use implicit many-to-many for simple join tables or explicit join models when extra fields are needed on the relation.
Do: Define relations explicitly with @relation annotations specifying fields and references and add onDelete cascade where appropriate
Dont: Skip @relation annotations or manually create join tables without Prisma relation support

Description: Define enums in your Prisma schema to constrain field values at the database level. Use enum types for status fields, roles, and categories to ensure data integrity and get TypeScript type safety.
Do: Define enums in schema.prisma for constrained value sets and use them as field types in models
Dont: Use plain String fields for values that should be constrained or define enums only in TypeScript without database enforcement

Description: Use Prisma's generated type-safe CRUD methods (create, findUnique, findMany, update, delete) instead of raw SQL. The generated client provides autocompletion, compile-time type checking, and prevents typos in field names.
Do: Use Prisma's generated CRUD methods with proper where clauses and let TypeScript catch field name errors at compile time
Dont: Use raw queries for standard CRUD operations or cast types to any to bypass type checking

Guideline: Filtering with where clause (contains, startsWith, in)
Description: Use Prisma's typed where clause with operators like contains, startsWith, endsWith, in, gt, gte, lt, lte, and logical combinators AND, OR, NOT for complex filtering at the database level.
Do: Build typed where objects using Prisma.ModelWhereInput and use mode insensitive for case-insensitive searches
Dont: Fetch all records and filter in JavaScript application code

Description: Use cursor-based pagination for infinite scroll and large datasets. Use offset-based (skip/take) for numbered pages. Always add a deterministic tiebreaker in orderBy to prevent duplicate or missing items across pages.
Do: Use cursor-based pagination with take and cursor for large datasets and offset pagination with skip and take plus a count query for numbered pages
Dont: Fetch all records and paginate in application code or use offset pagination without a tiebreaker field

Guideline: Select and Include for field selection
Description: Use select to fetch only specific fields, reducing data transfer. Use include to load relations alongside the main model. Never combine select and include at the same level. Use nested select within include for fine-grained control.
Do: Use select to pick only needed fields and include with nested select to control which relation fields are loaded
Dont: Fetch entire models without select or deeply nest include loading the entire object graph

Description: Prisma supports creating parent and child records in a single atomic operation using nested create, createMany, and connectOrCreate within the data argument. This ensures consistency without manual transactions.
Do: Use nested create and connectOrCreate within a single write operation to atomically create related records
Dont: Create parent and child records in separate queries without transaction guarantees

Description: Use connect to link a new record to an existing related record by its unique identifier. Use connectOrCreate to link if exists or create if not. This avoids manual lookups and extra queries.
Do: Use connect with a unique identifier to link existing records and connectOrCreate when the related record may or may not exist
Dont: Manually query for the related record ID then pass it as a raw foreign key value

Description: Use interactive transactions (async callback form) when operations depend on intermediate read results. The callback receives a transaction client (tx) that must be used for all operations within the transaction boundary.
Do: Use prisma.$transaction with an async callback and use the tx client for all reads and writes within the transaction
Dont: Read data outside the transaction then write inside it creating a race condition window

Description: Use the array form of $transaction to batch independent write operations that must all succeed or all fail. Prisma executes them sequentially and rolls back on any failure.
Do: Use prisma.$transaction with an array of operations for atomic batches of independent writes
Dont: Execute multiple dependent writes without any transaction guarantees

Description: Use prisma migrate dev during development to generate SQL migration files and apply them. Use prisma migrate deploy in CI/CD and production to apply pending migrations without generating new ones. Never use db push in production.
Do: Use prisma migrate dev locally to generate migration files and prisma migrate deploy in CI/CD pipelines to apply them
Dont: Use prisma db push in production or manually edit generated migration SQL files

Description: Create a seed script in prisma/seed.ts to populate development and test databases with initial data. Use upsert operations to make the seed idempotent so it can be run multiple times safely.
Do: Create an idempotent seed script using upsert and configure it in package.json under the prisma.seed key
Dont: Use create in seed scripts which fail on re-run due to unique constraint violations

Description: Use $queryRaw for SELECT queries and $executeRaw for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE when Prisma's API cannot express the needed SQL. Always use tagged template literals for automatic parameterization to prevent SQL injection.
Do: Use tagged template literals with $queryRaw and $executeRaw for parameterized queries that prevent SQL injection
Dont: Concatenate user input into raw SQL strings or use $queryRawUnsafe with string interpolation

Guideline: Prisma middleware for soft delete, logging
Description: Use prisma.$use() to register middleware that intercepts queries before execution. Common use cases include soft delete (converting delete to update), automatic audit logging, and query performance monitoring.
Do: Use $use middleware for cross-cutting concerns like soft delete filtering and query timing applied once and enforced everywhere
Dont: Manually add deletedAt checks and timing logic to every individual query in the codebase

Description: Prisma throws typed errors with specific error codes (P2002, P2025, P2003, etc.). Catch PrismaClientKnownRequestError and inspect the code property to return meaningful HTTP responses instead of generic 500 errors.
Do: Check error instanceof PrismaClientKnownRequestError and switch on error.code to return appropriate HTTP status codes and messages
Dont: Catch all errors generically without inspecting Prisma error codes or let errors propagate as unhandled 500s

Description: Error code P2002 indicates a unique constraint violation. Extract the target fields from error.meta to tell the user exactly which field caused the conflict. Handle this specifically in create and update operations.
Do: Extract the violating field names from error.meta.target and return a specific conflict error message to the user
Dont: Return a generic error message that does not indicate which field caused the unique constraint violation

Description: Prisma does not support lazy loading. All relation data must be explicitly requested via include or select. Plan your queries to load exactly the relations you need upfront to avoid multiple round trips.
Do: Explicitly include or select relations you need in the initial query to load them in one database round trip
Dont: Assume relations are automatically loaded or make separate queries for each relation after the initial fetch

Description: The N+1 problem occurs when you query N records then make a separate query for each record's relations. Prisma solves this with include and select which generate efficient SQL JOINs or batched queries.
Do: Use include or select to load relations in the initial findMany query or use findMany with an in-filter for batch loading
Dont: Loop through findMany results and query related data one by one in a map or forEach

Description: Create a deeply mocked PrismaClient using jest-mock-extended for unit tests. Inject the mock via dependency injection so services can be tested without a real database connection.
Do: Use jest-mock-extended mockDeep to create a typed PrismaClient mock and inject it into services for isolated unit tests
Dont: Run unit tests against a real database making tests slow and flaky and dependent on external infrastructure

Description: Prisma generates TypeScript types for all models, input types, and return payloads. Use Prisma.ModelCreateInput for function parameters and Prisma.ModelGetPayload for return types with specific includes.
Do: Use Prisma-generated types like Prisma.UserCreateInput and Prisma.UserGetPayload for function signatures to maintain schema-to-code synchronization
Dont: Define manual TypeScript interfaces that duplicate and eventually drift from the Prisma schema

Description: Use Prisma.validator to create reusable, type-safe query argument objects. This ensures query fragments like select, where, and include are validated against the schema at compile time.
Do: Use Prisma.validator to define reusable typed query fragments that are compile-time validated against your schema
Dont: Build query argument objects as plain untyped objects that are not validated until runtime

Description: In production deployments, use prisma migrate deploy to apply pending migrations. This command never generates new migrations and is safe for automated pipelines. Always run prisma generate before building the application.
Do: Run prisma migrate deploy in your CI/CD pipeline before starting the application and run prisma generate as a postinstall hook
Dont: Run prisma migrate dev in production or use prisma db push in automated deployments
